g.data-narration: Welcome to IoT Levels and Deployment Templates. We'll explore how IoT systems range from simple single devices to complex cloud-edge hybrids.
IoT systems vary enormously in complexity. Understanding levels helps engineers select the right architecture for their application's requirements.
g.text: IoT systems range from simple single devices to complex cloud-edge ecosystems.
Levels help select appropriate architecture balancing cost, complexity, and performance.

g.data-narration: The six IoT levels progress from simple single-device systems to complex hybrid edge-cloud architectures with distributed computing capabilities.
g.text: Single Device → Multiple Nodes | Local → Cloud → Hybrid

g.data-narration: Level 1 is the simplest form - a single IoT device performs all functions including sensing, processing, storage, and communication.
g.text: Simplest form - one device performs all functions

g.data-narration: Level 2 adds local storage capability to the single node, enabling historical data analysis and offline operation.
g.text: Level 2: Single Node with Local Storage
Adds local data storage for historical analysis and offline operation

g.data-narration: Level 3 incorporates edge analytics at the device itself, enabling smarter decision-making without cloud dependency and faster response times.
g.text: Level 3: Single Node with Local Storage and Analytics
Edge computing capabilities - local data analytics, reduced cloud dependency

g.data-narration: Level 4 introduces multiple sensing nodes communicating with a central node for aggregation, storage, and local network processing.
g.text: Level 4: Multiple Nodes with Local Storage
Multiple sensors communicate with central node for aggregation

g.data-narration: Level 5 features multiple nodes sending data directly to cloud storage for centralized processing and scalable analytics.
g.text: Level 5: Multiple Nodes with Cloud Storage
Cloud-centric architecture - centralized storage and processing

g.data-narration: Level 6 is the most complex - combining edge computing for immediate response with cloud analytics for comprehensive long-term insights.
g.text: Level 6: Multiple Nodes with Cloud + Edge Computing
Hybrid architecture - edge for real-time, cloud for analytics

g.data-narration: Now let's explore deployment templates that provide standardized approaches for implementing IoT solutions based on specific requirements.
g.text: Four standardized approaches for implementing IoT solutions

g.data-narration: Cloud-Centric offers scalable storage and analytics but relies on network connectivity. Edge-Centric provides low latency and offline capability but with limited analytics scope.
Fog Computing balances workload between edge and cloud. Hybrid offers maximum flexibility but with highest complexity and integration challenges.
g.text: Template Comparison: Fog Computing vs Hybrid

g.data-narration: When designing IoT systems, consider scalability, network constraints, security, cost factors, and data management needs to choose the right level.

g.text: Data Management - Real-time vs analytics needs

g.data-narration: Here's a quick reference comparing all six levels by nodes, storage, processing type, complexity, and typical use cases.
Remember: Levels progress from simple to complex. Match your level to requirements considering latency, bandwidth, cost, and scalability. Edge for real-time, cloud for analytics.

g.text: • Levels 1→6: Simple single device → Complex hybrid edge-cloud
• Edge processing: Low latency, offline capable, real-time needs
• Cloud processing: Scalable, analytics, big data
